title: IMF Executive Board Concludes 2025 Article IV Consultation with China
body: The IMF described China's economy as resilient in 2025 because exports and policy stimulus held growth near target, but it also emphasized that private domestic demand remained weak and deflationary pressures persisted. The release pointed to slower growth in 2026, muted inflation, and downside risks tied to property weakness and elevated debt. It argued that more expansionary macro support, including further monetary easing, may still be needed while China rebalances toward consumption-led growth.

title: China sets a lower economic growth target of 4.5% to 5% for 2026 as challenges loom
body: Public reporting around the annual government work report stressed that China lowered its growth target range for 2026 and acknowledged ongoing domestic challenges, especially weak consumption and a fragile property backdrop. The narrative emphasized slower but still positive growth, subdued inflation pressure, and a policy stance that remains supportive as officials try to stabilize expectations and boost domestic demand.

title: China's Economy Off to Steady Start in 2026 Amid Lowered Expectations
body: The public market narrative highlighted a steady start to 2026, with industrial output and investment holding up, but it also stressed that the property sector remained weak and consumer sentiment was still soft. The overall tone was that growth is resilient rather than booming, inflation is still subdued, and policymakers are leaning toward continued support instead of a restrictive stance.

body:
